title: The Personal Guarantee Conversation Most Business Owners Never Think to Reopen
linkedin:
Signed a personal guarantee when you first borrowed for the business? Chances are it still says exactly what it said then, even if nothing else about the business does.

A guarantee gets put in place when a business is young: thin trading history, modest assets, not quite enough on its own numbers to satisfy a credit committee. That is standard practice, not a reflection on the owner.

What is less standard is what happens next. Years pass. EBITDA strengthens. The balance sheet fills out with real plant, stock or property. And the guarantee sits there, unchanged, because banks have no particular incentive to revisit an arrangement that is already working in their favour. Someone has to raise it.

The natural moment is a facility renewal or refinance, ideally backed by two to three years of financials stronger than when the guarantee was first signed. Walk in with a clear picture of serviceability and asset coverage, and you give the lender an easy basis to say yes to a reduced or released guarantee, rather than asking them to take it on faith.

There is also a version of this that surfaces later than expected: at exit. An outstanding personal guarantee has to be dealt with before a sale can settle, and unwinding one mid-transaction is slower and more complicated than doing it in the ordinary course of business. Easy to fix early. Awkward to fix late.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #FractionalCFO #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Quick one for business owners who've had a bank loan for a few years now: when did you last look at what your personal guarantee actually says?

Most guarantees get signed early, when the business is new and the bank needs extra comfort. Years later, the business is stronger but the guarantee is often exactly the same, because nobody's gone back and asked the bank to look again.

It usually only becomes urgent when a sale is on the table, and by then it's a much more complicated conversation to have.

Have you ever gone back to your bank to renegotiate a personal guarantee as the business grew? Curious how that conversation went for you.

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for East Coast business owners this week, particularly if you've been trading with the same bank facility for a few years.

Most personal guarantees get put in place early, when a business is still building its trading history and the bank needs extra comfort beyond the numbers. That's a normal part of borrowing for the first time. What's less normal is how rarely that guarantee gets looked at again once the business has grown, because banks have little reason to revisit an arrangement that already works in their favour.

The practical moment to raise it is at facility renewal or refinance, backed by two or three years of stronger financials than when the guarantee was first signed. It also matters more than most owners expect if a sale is ever on the horizon, since an outstanding guarantee has to be resolved before a transaction can settle.

We work with owner-led businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria on exactly this kind of banking and facility review. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
